title: G3P: Gradient-Guided Privacy-Preserving Pruning via Train-Test Gradient Saliency
description: We propose G3P, a neural network pruning method that explicitly identifies and removes privacy-leaking neurons through a novel privacy saliency metric based on train-test gradient magnitude differences. Unlike magnitude-based pruning where privacy improvements are incidental, G3P measures gradient magnitude differences between training and test data to identify neurons contributing to membership inference vulnerability. This privacy-specific formulation achieves more consistent privacy-utility-efficiency trade-offs than standard pruning methods.
motivation: While Wang et al. (IJCAI 2021) showed that magnitude-based pruning can reduce MIA attack accuracy by 10-13.6%, Yuan & Zhang (USENIX Security 2022) demonstrated that pruning can also increase privacy risks in certain scenarios. This inconsistency raises the question: can we design pruning strategies that reliably improve privacy? We hypothesize that neurons exhibiting significantly different gradient magnitudes between training and test data are the primary contributors to membership inference vulnerability. Our key insight is that this train-test gradient difference is a principled, privacy-specific criterion that has not been previously explored for neuron selection during pruning.
proposed_approach: G3P introduces a privacy saliency metric that quantifies how much each neuron contributes to membership inference vulnerability by measuring the absolute difference in gradient magnitudes between training and test data. The combined saliency score balances task importance (first-order Taylor expansion following Molchanov et al., 2019) and privacy risk (train-test gradient difference magnitude). Progressive structured pruning removes privacy-vulnerable channels, followed by fine-tuning with KL-divergence regularization to align training/test predictions. Our key distinction from Yuan & Zhang is that we use privacy-specific gradient-based saliency to SELECT which neurons to prune, whereas they apply KL regularization uniformly without neuron selection.
key_innovations: Novel privacy saliency metric based on train-test gradient magnitude differences - a privacy-specific formulation distinct from task-oriented gradient-based pruning
Combined task-privacy saliency scoring that explicitly targets privacy-leaking neurons for structured pruning
Systematic evaluation showing gradient-based neuron selection provides privacy gains beyond magnitude pruning + KL regularization combined

related_work.paper: Against Membership Inference Attack: Pruning is All You Need (Wang et al., IJCAI 2021)
related_work.relevance: Showed magnitude-based pruning can reduce MIA attack accuracy by 10-13.6%. Our work differs by explicitly targeting privacy-leaking neurons through train-test gradient saliency rather than relying on incidental effects.

related_work.paper: Membership Inference Attacks and Defenses in Neural Network Pruning (Yuan & Zhang, USENIX Security 2022)
related_work.relevance: Showed pruning can increase memorization and proposed KL-divergence regularization as defense. CRITICAL DISTINCTION: Yuan & Zhang apply KL regularization uniformly without pruning criterion modification. Our work introduces neuron-level privacy saliency that SELECTS which neurons to prune based on train-test gradient differences. We also add a hybrid baseline (magnitude pruning + KL) to isolate the value of gradient-based selection.

related_work.paper: PriPrune: Quantifying and Preserving Privacy in Pruned Federated Learning (Chu et al., ACM TOMPECS 2024)
related_work.relevance: Privacy-aware pruning for FL with pseudo-pruning masks. Our work targets general MIAs in centralized settings (not FL gradients) and uses gradient-based saliency rather than heuristic defense masks.

related_work.paper: Importance Estimation for Neural Network Pruning (Molchanov et al., CVPR 2019)
related_work.relevance: Introduced gradient-based importance using first-order Taylor expansion for task performance. Our work extends this framework to privacy by introducing train-test gradient differences as a separate privacy-specific saliency component.

related_work.paper: Membership Inference Attacks from First Principles (Carlini et al., IEEE S&P 2022)
related_work.relevance: State-of-the-art LiRA attack method. We use this for final privacy validation.

related_work.paper: Deep Learning with Differential Privacy (Abadi et al., ACM CCS 2016)
related_work.relevance: Standard DP-SGD baseline. Our method aims to achieve comparable privacy with better utility and no noise injection.

hypothesis: A saliency-guided pruning approach that identifies and removes neurons based on train-test gradient magnitude differences can achieve better and more consistent privacy-utility-efficiency trade-offs than magnitude-based pruning. Specifically, gradient-based neuron selection provides privacy benefits beyond what can be achieved by combining magnitude pruning with KL regularization.
testable_predictions: At 50% sparsity, G3P will reduce MIA AUC by 10-15% compared to magnitude pruning while maintaining comparable accuracy

G3P will outperform a hybrid baseline (magnitude pruning + KL regularization), demonstrating that gradient-based selection provides value beyond the combination of magnitude pruning and KL regularization
G3P will achieve similar privacy protection (MIA AUC ≈ 0.55-0.60) as DP-SGD with ε=4 but with 5-8% higher task accuracy

confirmed: G3P achieves statistically significantly lower MIA AUC than magnitude pruning at same sparsity (p < 0.05)
G3P outperforms hybrid baseline (magnitude + KL), confirming gradient-based selection adds value
G3P maintains within 2% of unpruned model accuracy at 30-50% sparsity
refuted: Train-test gradient difference saliency does not correlate better with MIA vulnerability than magnitude
Hybrid baseline (magnitude + KL) matches G3P performance, suggesting gradient selection adds no value
Pruning privacy-vulnerable neurons harms accuracy disproportionately (>5% drop at 30% sparsity)

baselines: Taylor Importance Pruning (Molchanov et al., 2019) - task-only gradient saliency
Hybrid: Magnitude Pruning + KL Regularization (isolates value of gradient selection)
attacks: LiRA (Carlini et al., 2022) for final validation

computational_feasibility: Streamlined experiments focused on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18. Time breakdown: baseline training (~2h), G3P experiments (~3h), baseline comparisons including hybrid (~1.5h), final LiRA validation (~1.5h). Total: ~8 hours on single A6000 (48GB). Threshold attack used for intermediate evaluations to save time; LiRA reserved for final validation only.
